content.problemStatement:
Given the rating RRR of a person, tell which division he belongs to. The rating range for each division are given below:



Division 111: 2000≤2000 \le2000≤ Rating.




Division 222: 1600≤1600 \le1600≤ Rating <2000\lt 2000<2000.




Division 333: Rating <1600\lt 1600<1600.




Input Format

The first line of the input contains TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each testcase contains a single line of input, which contains a single integer RRR.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a single line the answer: 111 if the person belongs to Division 111, 222 if the person belongs to Division 222, and 333 if the person belongs to Division 333.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer R, the rating of a person.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing 1 if the rating belongs to Division 1, 2 if it belongs to Division 2, or 3 if it belongs to Division 3.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Rating 1500 < 1600 → Division 3 (output 3).\nRating 4000 >= 2000 → Division 1 (output 1).\nRating 1900 is in [1600,2000) → Division 2 (output 2).

solution.approach: For each rating R we only need to check which interval it falls into. The intervals are mutually exclusive and cover all possible ratings: if R >= 2000 the answer is 1; else if R >= 1600 the answer is 2; otherwise the answer is 3. This is O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each of the T test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
